item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I first was very creeped out by a video SCP that featured a malevolent sentience observing and communicating to the viewer, and this article gave me similar chills. This is my favorite kind of SCP because it blurs reality between viewing and being a part of the anomaly - which is what we readers are doing, too! The fear hits close to home.

I keep wondering what happened to the Foundation man who vanished for 5 years! He felt sure those woods housed some life form. How could anyone explore the plane and not be utterly terrified?

My overall feeling is that some looping ritual is taking place in this house that is holding back something worse, and the "actors" are visitors to the dimension, too. Possibly trapped. We see the Young Woman was not allowed to outrun her fate, she was supernaturally forced back into the house to (eventually) become the next Old Woman in a veil.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. The article managed to capture the feel of early horror films (The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari, Nosferatu, etc.) without sacrificing its value as a strange anomaly. Great job with this, enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

As someone who's seen a fair share of silent and early horror films, I really liked this one. Seeing the Edison's Frankenstein image and saying "Hey, I recognize that!" was fun too. If anyone wants to watch a film similar to this skip- go with Vampyre. It's a silent, experimental film that reminded me a lot like this one.

It appears that there's a lot of subtext in this article too. Very vague, but the haziness of the article fits well with the concept, and I'm able to put things together for the most part. I'd love to hear more about Archibald, the Forrest Corridor, and the bones that were discovered under the floorboards. The theory about the characters corresponding to the people that have used the skip might hold weight too.

Duke Giroux: The Man- disturbed by the events going on in Plane 3084
Hermann Von Dietrich: The General- enjoys partaking in The Guests meal, WW2 mention
Archibald Rayne: The Old Woman/The Aristocrat- implied to be killed by the characters with the skeleton found in Dumont's report
Amelia Holmwood: Could also be the Old Woman, not sure.
Dr. Dumont: The Young Woman- focus on the Forrest
The Second Foundation visitor (not elaborated on?): The Boy?

Overall, a perfectly creepy article that leaves a lot up to interpretation. Clearly 1981 inspired, but it might even be…better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, and this richly deserved a +1. I'm not sure how ambiguous it's intended to be, but I'm rapt anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my working theory halfway through the article. Still not sure if it was intended or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hrm. Considering the cast of the film, I wonder if it might be possible to connect each user to a specific character. One the general, one the aristocrat, one the young man, and so on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The foundation thinks only six people have used this scip. The flim is titled "Seven Strangers at a Feast."

Something bad will happen to the next person who uses this, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Detail is great, and this one really scratched some historical itches. Old abstract films are a gold mine for stuff like this. Reminded me a bit of the story "Pickman's Other Model" by Caitlin Kiernan(just superficially, both have a shunned abstract silent film).

"Mechelen claimed to not remember any interactions with the Guest."

Great line after reading the synopsis. Really hits you, then the next interview confirms what should be obvious. Don't know how much I like that, but making it a "dream" recollection definitely works. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I've read enough O-5 stuff over the years that I should have level 4 access.

That aside, good skip. Lots of interesting "middle." The ending felt a little weak. It's not so much that it didn't tie things up, it's that it did so in a way that added nothing to my understanding. A twist would have been good. A resolution would have been good. This did neither. It just sort of… stopped. Is this a deliberate homage to old black and white movies? Either way, it combines with the hazy, sprawling, seemingly arbitrary plot threads and characters to sort of fog the brain in a manner very reminiscent of viewing an experimental silent film with sketchy context and unclear intent. In that sense, it works for me. I'm sure there's probably loads of references and subtext I'm not getting due to my unfamiliarity with early films, so consider this discussion post an outsider's perspective.

I liked it anyway! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love the imaginative use of old film stills to add to the creepiness of a report. It does have a Hanged King feel about it but that's all to the good. I'd like to hear more of Black Jakob.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really enjoyed this one I realised the film that mirrors imperialism in the time of its viewing (the aristocrat and the general urging the young boy to follow in their destructive footsteps, the young man not sure, the young woman fleeing and becoming the next quarry). As imperialism fades from the earth, so too do the entities.

Realising this pushed it past "another spooky video" territory for me and into something really, really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminded me of the Pitch Haven series for some reason, and I just can't shake the feeling that it's connected, even though I know it's not.

At any rate, it's an excellent article with a strong core narrative that never lost me once during the whole thing. Very well done +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wha, it seems someone brought an author back from the dead. And as always, even before i had an account, his writing blows my standards out the window.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. Am I the only one curious as to what Black Jakobs other works are? “Four Dot Four Dash” sounds particularly interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of Djoric's Video Oddity in a good way. I wanna know more about this motley crew.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really good SCP. However, if I could make one small suggestion. Make some mention that the instances of SCP-3084 must be kept in some kind of climate change or flame retardant room. Films of the silent era were made with nitrate film. This chemical compound both degraded overtime and was very prone to catching on fire, entire film archieves were burnt to the ground. So I just suggest a little procaution born out of non-anomolous reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's to much here to like, but what it sells it for me more than anything is the Foundation's detectivesque research on the item's background.

The Foundation is not just a professional organization by using some fancy words on their documents, and is always good to see them at play like this!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be your cache. Try bypassing: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Bypass_your_cache

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this quite a bit — there's a lot of decisions it makes which strike me as odd, but it's got an excellent atmosphere/mood to it, and the research journals are pretty excellent (the note about the author's other works — anomalous telegram, etc — was particularly great).

The images are all excellent — but that first one slapped me right out of my immersion. I could instantly recognize the "Noise" filter from GIMP/Photoshop. That being said, I think this is easily addressed: You can either (gently) blur the image, or just shrink its size (I think cutting in half would probably do it?). Anything that gets rid of that immediate "Oh, hey, look, it's the NOISE filter" reaction.

ETA: To clarify, when I look at it on my dekstop, it's fine (because the image is shrunk so small that I can't recognize the noise filter). It's when I look at it on my phone that it's big enough for me to immediately recognize it. Hence why I think shrinking it (to half size, or maybe even a little smaller — 300 pixel width?) would get rid of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really, really good. Really, really really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He has returned!

After an initial skim, I decided to +1. Will probably do a more in-depth reading later.

Edit: did not regret my +1

+ potential spoilers
– hide block

The last picture was honestly the most creeepy for me, and it acted as a marvelous punchline. It looks like somebody with a cloak pulled over their head. The last scene of SCP-3084 is the men pulling the old woman's cloak over the young woman's head.

But this creates more questions than answers for me. Where did they find her? Why does it look like she's in a lake? What's with the VCR stuff? Is this another film anomaly? 701? 993? 1981?

All in all, great skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thoroughly enjoyed this. I think the opening description kind of undersells what's coming next but once you start that deep dive it becomes unputdownable. As others have stated this is definitely a refreshing take on anomalous media, thoroughly exploring a specific subject after breaking it down rather than the usual slate of episodic logs. Very well done. I expected nothing less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've had a suspicion that the spooky movie/tv show/video game trope's already a little past its prime at this point, but this article still manages to remain fresh. It's an upvote from me, with the caveat that the article's punch doesn't really carry over after the initial synopsis/exploration — yes, there's the danger of 'they've disappeared/escaped', but I can't decide whether it's meant to be a less overt kind of sinking fridge horror, or it's just not presented heavily enough to be impactful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't see the images. Anyone know what's going on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s a bit derivative of 701 and 1981, but then, those are two of my favorite skips of all time so this is an easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn. I really enjoyed the film synopsis and the stills that accompany it, as they provide a great way for me as a reader to visualize this incredibly disturbing film. Additionally, the examples of things matching events from the movie added an additional chill factor.

+1. This is a really great example of an anomalous media skip, and just an all around great skip, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something about this reminds me of The Forbidden Room (an analysis of which can be found here) — maybe it's the fact you used actual stills from lost films, or the unsettling ways in which the film intersects with reality as we know it, or even the classic bbaztek-style CONTEXT REDACTED, but this works incredibly well for me. I'm a sucker for anomalous media skips and this delivers well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuuuuuuuuck, this rules. I love anomalous media with all my heart, and I love that somehow, a fucked-up film on the surface only gets more disturbing and weird as Hell, especially (or maybe even because?) of the era it was produced in. It never stopped being tense and continued to feel excellently paced. I very much adore this article.

item.title: Attribution
item.content:encoded:
Image 1 is my own work.

Image 2 is a still shot from She, lost 1917 film in the public domain.

Image 3 is unused footage from Metropolis, 1927 film in the public domain.

Image 4 is a still from Frankenstein, 1910 film in the public domain.

Image 5 is licensed under Wikimedia Commons and may be accessed here.

Image 6 is licensed under Wikimedia Commons and may be accessed here. Image manipulation my own work.

Image 7 is licensed under Wikimedia Commons and may be accessed here. Photo manipulation my own work.
